event: After the retreat of the last Ice Age glaciers, the first hunter-gatherers of the Kunda culture settle the territory of present-day Lithuania.
Neolithic Corded Ware and Globular Amphora cultures spread across the region, associated with the arrival of Indo-European peoples.
The ancestral Baltic tribes emerge, beginning to differentiate from other Indo-European groups along the southeastern Baltic coast.
The Roman historian Tacitus describes the Aestii, Baltic peoples who gather amber along the Baltic shore.
The name Lithuania (Litua) is first recorded in writing in the Annals of Quedlinburg, describing the death of the missionary Bruno of Querfurt.
Lithuanian forces crush the Livonian Brothers of the Sword at the Battle of Saule, halting German crusader expansion.
Grand Duke Mindaugas is baptized as a Christian, securing papal recognition of a Lithuanian kingdom.
Mindaugas is crowned the first and only King of Lithuania, founding the Lithuanian state.
Gediminas becomes Grand Duke and greatly expands the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, founding the Gediminid dynasty.
Vilnius is first mentioned in writing as the capital in letters sent by Grand Duke Gediminas inviting merchants and craftsmen.
The Union of Krewo binds Lithuania to Poland as Grand Duke Jogaila agrees to marry Queen Jadwiga and adopt Christianity.
Lithuania officially adopts Roman Catholicism, becoming the last pagan state in Europe to Christianize.
Combined Polish-Lithuanian forces under Vytautas and Jogaila decisively defeat the Teutonic Knights at the Battle of Grunwald (Tannenberg).
Grand Duke Vytautas the Great dies after expanding Lithuania to its greatest extent, stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea.
The First Statute of Lithuania is enacted, codifying one of Europe's most advanced legal systems.
The Union of Lublin merges Lithuania and Poland into the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, a single elective monarchy.
Vilnius University is founded by the Jesuits, becoming the oldest university in the Baltic region and northeastern Europe.
Russian and Swedish forces invade and devastate Lithuania during the wars of the mid-17th century, sacking Vilnius.
The Third Partition of Poland-Lithuania erases the Commonwealth, and most of Lithuania is annexed by the Russian Empire.
Lithuanians join the November Uprising against Russian rule, which is suppressed and followed by harsh reprisals.
The January Uprising against the Russian Empire sweeps Lithuania, leading to a ban on Lithuanian-language publications in the Latin alphabet.
The nationalist newspaper Auszra (Dawn) begins publication, igniting the modern Lithuanian national revival.
Russia lifts the ban on Lithuanian-language press, allowing a flourishing of national culture and publishing.
The Council of Lithuania proclaims the restoration of an independent Lithuanian state on 16 February.
Poland seizes Vilnius and the surrounding region, prompting Lithuania to make Kaunas its temporary capital.
Lithuania annexes the Klaipeda (Memel) region from League of Nations administration after a staged uprising.
A military coup brings Antanas Smetona to power, establishing an authoritarian nationalist regime.
Nazi Germany forces Lithuania to cede the Klaipeda region under threat of invasion.
The Soviet Union occupies and annexes Lithuania following the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact, creating the Lithuanian SSR.
Soviet mass deportations to Siberia begin, followed weeks later by Nazi German occupation and the Holocaust that destroyed most of Lithuania's Jewish community.
The Soviet Union reoccupies Lithuania, triggering a decade-long armed partisan resistance by the Forest Brothers.
Lithuanian partisan leaders sign a unified declaration proclaiming the Movement of the Struggle for the Freedom of Lithuania.
The Sajudis reform movement is founded, spearheading the drive for independence during the era of perestroika.
Around two million people form the Baltic Way human chain linking Vilnius, Riga, and Tallinn to demand independence.
Lithuania becomes the first Soviet republic to declare the restoration of its independence on 11 March.
Soviet troops storm the Vilnius TV tower on 13 January, killing 14 civilians, but Lithuania's independence is recognized internationally and it joins the United Nations.
Lithuania adopts a new democratic constitution by national referendum.
The litas is reintroduced as the national currency and the last Russian troops withdraw from Lithuanian soil.
Lithuania joins both NATO and the European Union, anchoring itself firmly in Western institutions.
Vilnius serves as a European Capital of Culture as Lithuania marks the millennium of its name's first written mention.
Lithuania adopts the euro as its currency, joining the eurozone.
Gitanas Nauseda is elected President of Lithuania, continuing the country's pro-Western and pro-European course.
Lithuania establishes a Taiwanese Representative Office in Vilnius, triggering a major diplomatic and economic rift with China.
Lithuania becomes a leading supporter of Ukraine after Russia's full-scale invasion, sharply boosting defense spending.
